attachment.definition: An emotional bond between people, especially between a baby and their caregiver.
attachment.example: A secure attachment helps a child feel safe in the world.

theory.definition: A set of ideas that explains how or why something works.
theory.example: Attachment theory explains how early bonds affect us.

advantage.definition: Something that puts you in a better position than others.
advantage.example: You have a huge advantage over your baby because you have experience.

witnessed.definition: Seen something happen with your own eyes.
witnessed.example: She witnessed other parents caring for their children.

consigned.definition: Put or sent somewhere to be kept or stored.
consigned.example: Your early memories are consigned to unconscious memory.

unconscious.definition: The part of your mind that stores memories and feelings you are not aware of.
unconscious.example: Many early experiences stay in our unconscious memory.

impression.definition: A strong effect or feeling that an experience leaves on your mind.
impression.example: First experiences form the deepest impressions.

philosophy.definition: A general way of thinking about or understanding something.
philosophy.example: Your early experiences shape your philosophy of people.

consolidated.definition: Made strong and firm over time; settled.
consolidated.example: Our view of people was consolidated long ago in childhood.

associations.definition: Mental connections between an experience and a feeling.
associations.example: That holiday has good associations because the people were kind.

fondly.example: She thought fondly of her childhood home.

calamities.example: Whatever calamities happen, they will recover more quickly.

befall.definition: To happen to someone, especially something bad.
befall.example: Whatever calamities befall them, they will be strong.

lovable.definition: Easy to love; worthy of being loved.
lovable.example: Every child needs to feel that they are lovable.

caregivers.definition: People who take care of someone, especially a child.
caregivers.example: A baby's earliest caregivers shape their view of the world.

desert.definition: A large area of very dry land with almost no water or plants.
desert.example: Imagine you are alone in a desert with nothing around you.

shelter.definition: A place that protects you from weather or danger.
shelter.example: In the desert, there was no food and no shelter.

berserk.definition: Wildly out of control; acting in a crazy way because of strong emotion.
berserk.example: You would go berserk trying to get their attention.

desperate.definition: Feeling that you have no hope and will try anything.
desperate.example: The lost hiker was desperate for water.

womb.definition: The part inside a mother's body where a baby grows before birth.
womb.example: The baby spent nine months growing in the womb.

synchronized.definition: Made to happen at the same time or work together perfectly.
synchronized.example: The womb is an environment synchronized to the baby's needs.

signal.definition: To communicate or show something through sounds or actions.
signal.example: After birth, the baby must signal their needs to us.

bodily.example: We learn to read the baby's bodily cues.

decipher.definition: To figure out the meaning of something that is hard to understand.
decipher.example: Parents learn to decipher what their baby's cries mean.

appropriately.definition: In a way that is suitable and correct for the situation.
appropriately.example: When we respond appropriately, the baby feels safe.

attuned.definition: Sensitive to and aware of someone's feelings or needs.
attuned.example: An attuned parent notices when their baby is hungry.

mismatched.definition: Not fitting together well; not matching the need.
mismatched.example: A mismatched response leaves the baby feeling confused.

attended.example: The baby screamed for a long time before being attended to.

default.definition: The standard or automatic way something happens unless changed.
default.example: Your early experiences become a default way of feeling.

pre-programmed.definition: Built in from birth; already set up to do something.
pre-programmed.example: Babies come into the world pre-programmed to form bonds.

needy.definition: Wanting too much attention or help from others.
needy.chinese: 需索無度的；過度依賴的
needy.example: He felt needy in relationships because of his childhood.

secure.definition: Feeling safe and confident; not worried about being abandoned.
secure.example: A secure child feels confident to explore the world.

insecure.definition: Not feeling safe or confident; worried about being rejected.
insecure.chinese: 不安全的；沒有安全感的
insecure.example: An insecure child may be anxious when their parent leaves.

ambivalent.definition: Having mixed or conflicting feelings about something.
ambivalent.example: She felt ambivalent about leaving her child at daycare.

avoidant.definition: Tending to stay away from close relationships or emotional connection.
avoidant.example: An avoidant person finds it hard to let others get close.

dismissive.definition: Treating others or their feelings as unimportant.
dismissive.example: A dismissive parent ignores their child's emotional needs.

foster.definition: To encourage or help something grow and develop.
foster.example: Parents want to foster a secure attachment in their baby.

deliberate.definition: Done on purpose; carefully thought about.
deliberate.example: She made a deliberate effort to bond with her child.

empathic.definition: Able to understand and share another person's feelings.
empathic.example: An empathic parent can sense what their baby needs.

sustenance.definition: Food and drink needed to stay alive and healthy.
sustenance.example: The baby's needs for closeness and sustenance must be met.

consistently.definition: In a regular way; the same way every time.
consistently.example: If needs are consistently met, the child feels safe.

optimistic.definition: Expecting good things to happen; having a positive view.
optimistic.example: Secure children tend to be more optimistic about life.

optimizes.definition: Makes the best possible; improves to the highest level.
optimizes.example: Feeling secure optimizes your chances in life.

permanence.definition: The state of lasting and not going away.
permanence.example: Object permanence is knowing something exists even when you cannot see it.

psychotherapists.definition: Professionals trained to help people with emotional and mental problems through talking.
psychotherapists.example: Psychotherapists use the term 'object permanence' for this concept.

inconsistently.definition: In a way that changes and is not the same each time; unreliably.
inconsistently.example: If a parent responds inconsistently, the child feels confused.

overlooked.example: The child felt overlooked when no one answered their cries.

companionship.definition: The state of having someone with you; being together with someone.
companionship.example: Babies need constant companionship to feel safe.

trustworthy.definition: Able to be trusted; reliable and honest.
trustworthy.example: Children need to learn that people are trustworthy.

blueprint.definition: A plan or pattern that guides how something is built or done.
blueprint.example: Your first experiences give you a blueprint for relationships.

supplant.definition: To take the place of something old with something new.
supplant.example: Positive experiences can supplant earlier negative patterns.

mantra.definition: A word or phrase repeated often that represents a belief.
mantra.example: His internal mantra became 'nobody cares about me.'

influence.definition: The power to change or affect something or someone.
influence.example: He did not believe he had influence over other people.

loner.definition: A person who prefers to be alone and avoids other people.
loner.example: Without close bonds, a child may grow up to be a loner.

empathy.definition: The ability to understand and share another person's feelings.
empathy.example: Growing up without love can lead to a lack of empathy.

conscience.definition: The part of you that knows what is right and wrong.
conscience.example: A difficult childhood can lead to a shaky moral conscience.

trace.definition: To follow back to the origin or source of something.
trace.example: Can you trace how these patterns were passed down in your family?

generations.definition: Groups of people born around the same time in a family line.
generations.example: These patterns were passed down through generations.

replicate.definition: To do again in the same way; to copy.
replicate.example: How will you replicate that feeling of security with your baby?

abused.definition: Treated in a cruel or violent way.
abused.example: Children who were abused may struggle to trust others.

shaky.example: He developed a shaky sense of right and wrong.

moral.definition: Related to what is right and wrong.
moral.example: Early care affects a child's moral development.
